coredata.dc:description: For the first time, the valorization of waste shells from the Siraitia grosvenorii industry involves their conversion into carbon dots (CDs) for the detection of deep eutectic solvents (DESs) concentrations through their fluorescence effect. The material composition of the fruit shells was first analyzed, revealing that lignin constitutes 29.8 % of their total mass. Then four types of DESs were prepared for the extraction of lignin from these shells; among them, choline chloride-lactic acid (ChCl-Lac) achieving the highest extraction rate of 92.49 %. The obtained lignin was characterized and subsequently used as a raw material to synthesize carbon dots possessing tunable fluorescent properties with a quantum yield of 5.73 %. Moreover, it was found that Fe3 + could quench the fluorescence of the CDs, while the addition of certain DESs could restore the quenched fluorescence. The presence of DESs alone could also induce changes in fluorescence when interacting with the CDs. Based on this effect, a new system for the effective quantitation of the concentration of DESs in aqueous solutions using CDs was established, and DESs were used in several main steps and played multiple roles. This study provides not only a solution for the value-added utilization of solid waste from current fruit industry, but also a new approach for the concentration detection of DESs in aqueous solutions.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon dots from Siraitia grosvenorii shells and their fluorescent response for deep eutectic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the first time, the valorization of waste shells from the Siraitia grosvenorii industry involves their conversion into carbon dots (CDs) for the detection of deep eutectic solvents (DESs) concentrations through their fluorescence effect. The material composition of the fruit shells was first analyzed, revealing that lignin constitutes 29.8 % of their total mass. Then four types of DESs were prepared for the extraction of lignin from these shells; among them, choline chloride-lactic acid (ChCl-Lac) achieving the highest extraction rate of 92.49 %. The obtained lignin was characterized and subsequently used as a raw material to synthesize carbon dots possessing tunable fluorescent properties with a quantum yield of 5.73 %. Moreover, it was found that Fe3 + could quench the fluorescence of the CDs, while the addition of certain DESs could restore the quenched fluorescence. The presence of DESs alone could also induce changes in fluorescence when interacting with the CDs. Based on this effect, a new system for the effective quantitation of the concentration of DESs in aqueous solutions using CDs was established, and DESs were used in several main steps and played multiple roles. This study provides not only a solution for the value-added utilization of solid waste from current fruit industry, but also a new approach for the concentration detection of DESs in aqueous solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) were first reported in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes using the so-called arc-discharge method [1–3]. CDs exhibit excellent properties, especially their photoluminescence, which can be tuned by controlling their size, surface state, and doping with various substances. The abundance of functional groups on their surface allows CDs to adsorb and interact with a wide range of targets, thereby endowing them with sensing capabilities [4–8]. In recent years, CDs have garnered significant attention in materials science, biotechnology, biomedicine, energy, and environmental applications [9–13]. As a valuable renewable resource, various biomass has been increasingly utilized to synthesize environmentally friendly and highly biocompatible CDs [14,15]. Extensive research and applications of such biomass-derived CDs have been reported. For instance, Kumar et al. utilized palm kernel shells and oyster shells to prepare CDs for cellular fluorescence imaging, demonstrating their potential in biomedical applications [16]. Basha and coworkers synthesized CDs from neem tree bark and used them to adsorb methylene blue, achieving an optimal adsorption rate of 64.7 % [17]. Zhang’s group prepared CDs from goji berries for the quantitative detection of the food dye sunset yellow in beverages [18]. Most recently, a white fluorescent CDs was developed for detecting Fe3+, that can emit fluorescence across the full wavelength range by adjusting the solvent type and pH [19]. As a whole, biomass-resulted CDs have been widely applied in fluorescence imaging, adsorption, and detection fields. However, their application in the detection field is currently limited, with a large portion being used to detect metal ions, and their fluorescence properties after quenching by metal ions have not been further discussed. Meanwhile, the use of CDs in the field of green solvents is still quite limited, which needs further extension.

As a novel class of green solvents, DESs are mixtures composed of two or more components with strong hydrogen bonding interactions, resulting in a melting point lower than that of the individual components [20]. Due to simple preparation, low cost, excellent solubility, and low volatility, DESs have shown promising application potential in areas such as catalytic reactions, extraction and separation, electrodeposition, and nanomaterial synthesis [21–24]. In another study, a kind of DES prepared with urea and ammonium thiocyanate was successfully applied to prepare cellulose nanofibril films with favorable thermal stability and mechanical performance [25], indicating DES also has great potential in the cellulose industry just like ionic liquids (ILs). At the same time, the used amount of DES reached 100 times the pulp as raw material in this case, which means that the consumption of DES would be very great in large scale, and some problems that do not need to be considered in small-scale trials will arise. Although DESs can be recovered post-use, complete recovery is not achievable. On one hand, those released in water may lead to eutrophication and environmental risks [26]. On the other hand, solvent residue is a highly anticipated issue, especially for those products in the researches belonging to food and pharmaceutical fields. Therefore, the development of effective detection techniques for DESs is both necessary and crucial. Unfortunately, research in this area lags far behind basic and applied studies on DESs at present, as well as the quantitation studies of ILs. Current methods for detecting DES are somewhat limited, with high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) being the most common approach, meanwhile many DESs (and their components) do not have strong UV absorbance. Actually, the fluorescence effect of CDs is well-suited for DES concentration detection. Fluorescence detection is rapid, and using CDs for DES concentration measurement does not require organic solvents, making it environmentally friendly.

As a plant unique to China, Siraitia grosvenorii has garnered significant attention for its rich nutritional and medicinal values as both a food and medicinal crop. The rapid development of its associated cultivation and secondary industries has led to widespread applications in the fields of food and beverage, health products, and pharmaceuticals [27–30]. Song et al. explored the anti-asthmatic effects of mogroside V (Mog V) in ovalbumin (OVA)-induced asthmatic micereduced which could effectively reduce the number of inflammatory cells in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) [31]. Mahmoud et al. conducted a preliminary investigation into the antibacterial activity of Siraitia grosvenorii leaves, identifying notable inhibitory effects against Streptococcus mutans [32]. Further mechanistic studies suggested that the antibacterial action may be due to the inhibition of glucosyltransferase. However, the industrial applications of raw materials from Siraitia grosvenorii have been largely confined to food and drug sectors, with substantial resource wastage occurring during production, such as the discarding of a huge amount of fruit shells. The utilization of these waste materials is of great significance. As one of the main byproducts of Siraitia grosvenorii, there has been limited attention given to its shells, which are mainly burned, with a small amount used in the fields of animal feed and crop fertilizers. There have been no reports of its use in analytical fields, nor any research related to deep eutectic solvents. Related research urgently needs to be carried out.

Under above background, this study aimed to diversify the use of Siraitia grosvenorii resources by preparing lignin from the discarded fruit shells and further converting it into CDs, and DESs were used in several main steps and played multiple roles. The fluorescent properties of these CDs were explored, and they were ultimately used for the detection of DES. Through a series of comprehensive studies of analysis and pretreatment of raw material, lignin extraction, CDs preparation, basic characterization, properties testing and application validation, it was expected to provide a solution for the high-value utilization of biomass resources, turning waste into treasure; secondly, it offered a new technical method for the rapid quantitation of representative DESs. Due to the complexity of the whole contents, Fig. 1 is especially applied to illustrate the technical roadmap of this study to make readers clear.

All the reagents and solvents used in the present study were of analytical-reagent grade or higher, which were used directly without special treatment unless otherwise specified. Phenol, anhydrous ethanol, sodium sulfite, lauric acid, bromacetyl, and perchloric acid were obtained from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China), while glacial acetic acid, concentrated sulfuric acid, thymol, choline chloride, and deionized water were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Experimental deionized water was sourced from an ultra-pure (UP) water purification system (0.4 μm filter) manufactured by Millipore Co., Ltd., (Bedford, USA). The discarded shells from roasted fruits of Siraitia grosvenorii were collected in Yongfu county (Guilin, China), which was ground into powders and store in dryer for further use.

In this study, 380 type UV-Vis spectrophotometer (AOE Instrumental Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and 960PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Yidian Analysis Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) wereapplied to measure the ultraviolet and fluorescence properties, respectively. Spectrum Two L1600300 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Perkin Elmer, Waltham, USA) was employed to explore chemical composition of DES. Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern Panalytical Co., Ltd., UK) was used to determine the particle size of CDs. JNM-ECZ400S/L1 nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (JEOL Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was applied to analyze the atomic spectra of DES, lignin, and CDs. K-ALPHA elemental analyzer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, USA) was used to analyze the elemental composition of lignin and CDs. ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fischer, Waltham, USA) was employed to characterize the products. Besides, STA 449 C instrument (Netzsch, Geraetebau, Deutschland) was used to discover the pyrolysis characteristics of lignin.PL-type gel permeation chromatography (GPC, Agilent Technologies Inc., Santa Clara, USA) was applied to analyze the molecular weight of lignin. JEM-F200 scanning electrical microscope (JEOL Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) was used to observe the lattice morphology and particle size of CDs.

The preparation method for DES involves mixing the two components of H-bonding acceptor and H-bonding donor in a specific molar ratio, followed by stirring the mixture at a constant temperature for a predetermined duration [33]. After screened from pilot experiments, four types of DESs were finally used for lignin extraction, with their abbreviations and synthesis conditions as follows: ChCl-Lac (choline chloride:lactic acid = 1:6, 130°C, 2 h), ChCl-But (choline chloride:1,4-butanediol = 1:4, 80°C, 4 h), ChCl-Imi (choline chloride:imidazole = 3:7, 130°C, 4 h), and ChCl-Mon (choline chloride:monoethanolamine = 1:7, 80°C, 2 h). Moreover, four types of DESs were used to investigate the effects on the fluorescence of CDs, with their abbreviations and synthesis conditions as follows: ChCl-Mon (choline chloride:monoethanolamine = 1:7, 80°C, 2 h), ChCl-Urea (choline chloride:urea = 1:2, 65°C, 1 h), ChCl-Imi (choline chloride:imidazole = 3:7, 130°C, 4 h), and Lys-Gly (lysine:glycerol = 1:8, 80°C, 2 h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Component analysis and pretreatment of Siraitia grosvenoriishells
ce:section.ce:para: The composition of Siraitia grosvenorii shells is not clearly defined, which necessitates a compositional analysis for the following application. The shells were first powdered (60 mesh), followed by the determination of moisture content, ash content, benzene-ethanol extractives, cellulose, acid-soluble lignin, and acid-insoluble lignin as the common indexes of such a natural biomass [34–38]. Considering the presence of lipids that may affect the quality of the extracted lignin, two methods were employed to remove these coexisting components, including the benzene-ethanol extraction and the DES-involved treatment [39]. A comparative analysis of the removal efficiencies of both methods was performed. The specific measurement details are provided in the Supplementary Material (SM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lignin from Siraitia grosvenorii shells with DESs
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to previous procedures [40–43], the synthesized DESs were mixed with the shell powders of Siraitia grosvenorii in a 20:1 mass ratio (g/g), and then stirred in an oil bath at 120°C for 6 hours. Upon cooling, the mixture was centrifuged to isolate the supernatant, which was then mixed with 4 L of deionized water. The precipitate was washed with ethanol, centrifuged again to recover the supernatant, and the solvent of ethanol was removed under vacuum to yield a residual liquid. Then this liquid was transferred into a large conical flask containing an additional 4 L of deionized water, where it was allowed to stand for 48 hours to complete lignin precipitation. The lignin precipitate was then filtered and freeze-dried for 12 hours, and the dried product was collected for further characterization and use.

0.1 g of lignin product was placed in a 10 mL stainless steel hydrothermal reactor, followed by the addition of 10 mL of deionized water. The mixture was then heated at a constant temperature of 210°C for 4 hours. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. It was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 minutes to separate the solid phase from the liquid [44]. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm water-soluble filter to yield a yellow-brown lignin-resulted CDs solution. For the further characterizations and application, the solution was freeze-dried for 48 hours to obtain the final product of CDs powders.

The absorbance of quinine sulfate and CDs solutions were first measured and adjusted using UV-Vis spectroscopy to ensure they matched. Following this, the fluorescence emission spectra of both solutions were determined at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. CDs, which emit blue fluorescence under UV light, were analyzed in comparison to quinine sulfate. Quinine sulfate, which was dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 solution with a known quantum yield of 54 %, was selected as the standard reference for the quantum yield determination of the CDs. The calculation formula is expressed as Eq. (1):

The prepared CDs solution was diluted by half, and its emission and excitation spectra were subsequently measured using the spectrofluorometer. For the emission spectrum measurement, emission spectra were recorded at various excitation wavelengths, ranging from 300 to 480 nm in 20 nm increments. For each excitation wavelength, the emission spectrum was determined over the range of 200–700 nm. The scanning speed was set at 1000 nm/min, with a scanning interval of 1 nm, an excitation bandwidth of 5 nm, and an emission bandwidth of 10 nm. The photomultiplier tube (PMT) gain was set to medium at 650 V.

For the excitation spectrum measurement, the emission wavelength was fixed at 460 nm, with the excitation wavelength scanned from 200 to 450 nm. The scanning speed was set to 1000 nm/min, with a scanning interval of 1 nm. The excitation bandwidth was 5 nm, and the emission bandwidth was 10 nm. The photomutiplier tubes (PMT) gain was set to medium at 650 V [46]. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching and recovery effects of CDs solutions were examined from several perspectives, including the impact of various metal ions (Fe3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Ca2+), the influence of different Fe3+ concentrations (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10 mmol/L), the effects of four distinct DES solutions, the recovery of fluorescence in CDs/Fe3+ mixtures by these DES solutions, and the restoration of fluorescence in different CDs/metal ion mixtures upon treatment with DES. The fluorescence emission peak variations of CDs under different solvent conditions with pH changes were analyzed, and their reusability was also evaluated after repeated use. Detailed methods are provided in SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lignin-resulted CDs for concentration detection of DES
ce:section.ce:para: Two methods were explored for detecting the concentration of DES in aqueous solutions using CDs solutions according to previous studies [47]. The first method involved preparing a CDs/Fe3+ composite solution, where the fluorescence of the CDs had already been quenched by Fe3+. Upon the addition of the DES solution, the quenched fluorescence was restored, and the degree of fluorescence recovery could be used to quantitatively determine the DES concentration. The second method involved directly mixing the CDs solution with the DES and monitoring the resulting fluorescence change to quantify the DES concentration. The results were then compared with those obtained from HPLC for validation. Detailed methods are provided in SM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Component analysis of Siraitia grosvenorii fruit shell
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed composition analysis is crucial for understanding the raw material properties and necessary for the effective lignin utilization as well as fabrication of CDs. As the results, the basic composition of Siraitia grosvenorii fruit shells is shown in Fig. 2 A, revealing the concentrations of water, ash, oil-soluble components, acid-insoluble lignin, acid-soluble lignin, and holocellulose. The experimental results prove that holocellulose is the predominant component, accounting for 34.7 % of the total weight. Lignin follows as the second most abundant component with a total content of 29.8 %, consisting of 3.3 % acid-soluble lignin and 26.5 % acid-insoluble lignin. Together, these two components constitute the majority of the raw material composition. Additionally, the fruit shell contains 15.2 % oil-soluble components, 6.8 % moisture, 4.4 % ash, and 9.1 % other minor constituents. In comparison to other fruit shells, Siraitia grosvenorii shells have a higher lignin content. For instance, the lignin contents of pistachio shells, cashew shells, and tung shells are 15.8 %, 13.7 %, and 26.2 %, respectively [47,48]. Therefore, the utilization of its lignin is of greater significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lignin from Siraitia grosvenorii shells using DES
Pretreatment of removing lipids using DES
ce:section.ce:para: To effectively extract lignin from fruit shells of Siraitia grosvenorii, it is essential to remove lipid components, which can serve as troubled impurities in the extracted lignin. To avoid the unfriendly nature commonly associated with traditional organic solvents, the DES composed of natural components was chosen as the solvent for removing the lipid-soluble constituents. According to current literature [49], hydrophobic DESs always demonstrate highly effective dissolving capability for lipids. For example, it exhibited an extraction efficiency six times higher when using the DES of thymol-lactic acid (1:1) for lipid-soluble beta-carotene compared to the traditional organic solvent mixture of n-hexane-ethanol-water [50]. Additionally, the DES of choline chloride-malonic acid (1:1) demonstrated a more than five-fold higher extraction efficiency (10 μg/g) for total carotenoids in goji berries compared to the combination use of organic solvents (hexane:ethanol:acetone:toluene=10:6:6:7, v/v/v/v) and supercritical CO2 [51]. These findings confirm that the hydrophobic DES is both an effective and environmentally friendly extractant for lipid components. After optimized through pre-experiments, the DES was prepared by mixing lauric acid and thymol in a molar ratio of 4:5, followed by stirring at 80°C for 2 h in this study. The resulting DES is a clear and colorless liquid at room temperature, despite both the components being solid in their pure forms. FTIR and NMR analyses confirmed that the two components did not undergo chemical reactions but instead formed a eutectic mixture through hydrogen bonding interactions. Through analysis on Fig. S1 in SM, it shows that the absorbance peak of thymol at 3533 cm−1 shifted to 3562 cm−1 in the DES, while the absorption peak of lauric acid at 1701 cm−1 shifted to 1716 cm−1. This indicates the formation of hydrogen bonds between the two. Detailed results of the FTIR and NMR analyses are provided in SM. Above results suggest that the designed DES has been successfully prepared.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the DES method for removing oil-soluble impurities, it was compared with a traditional organic solvent approach using the mixture of benzene and ethanol. The results indicate that the impurity removal efficiency of the DES method is 22.02 %, slightly surpassing the 21.01 % efficiency achieved by the benzene-ethanol method. The primary advantage of the former lies in its use of green and low-risk solvent during the impurity removal process, making it a friendlier alternative for environment-healthy-safety (EHS).

DESs often possess unique solubility properties that enable them to selectively dissolve lignin, making them highly suitable to obtain such a natural polymer from biomass resources. Based on a literature review [52], four DESs known for their high lignin extraction efficiency were selected, including ChCl-Lac (1:6), ChCl-But (1:4), ChCl-Mon (1:7), and ChCl-Imi (3:7). Their extraction efficiencies for lignin are presented and compared in Fig. 2 B. It can be found that the DES of ChCl-Lac (1:6) demonstrates a significantly better performance compared to the other three for the lignin from siraitia grosvenorii shells, achieving an excellent extraction efficiency of 92.49 %. According to current reports, the DESs achieved lignin extraction efficiency of 64.3 % and 70.2 % for walnut shells and walnuts, respectively; and 88.0 % for oil palm fruit shells [52,53]. In comparison, ChCl-Lac (1:6) demonstrates a higher lignin extraction efficiency from natural raw material in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of lignin from Siraitia grosvenorii shells
ce:section.ce:para: After comparing the lignin extraction efficiencies of the four DES types, the DES of ChCl-Lac (1:6) was selected as the optimal extraction solvent. The extracted lignin was subsequently characterized using various techniques as follows. Firstly, the FTIR spectrum of lignin product is shown in Fig. 3 A. The broad peak at 3387 cm−1 corresponds to phenolic and alcoholic hydroxyl groups. The peak at 2925 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of methyl and methylene C-H bonds. The absorbance peak at 1736 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration of acetyl groups. The peak at 1662 cm−1 is due to the bending vibration of adsorbed water. Featured signals at 1512 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1 are contributed by the vibrational carbon skeleton of the benzene ring in lignin. The peak at 1209 cm−1 is due to C-O stretching vibration [54].

The TG results of lignin product are shown in Fig. 3 B, which indicates that lignin undergoes four stages of pyrolysis. The typical pyrolysis stages of lignin include the removal of free water, initial volatilization, and deep pyrolysis. The first stage of pyrolysis occurs between 85.5 °C and 189.6 °C, with a weight loss of 10.6 %. During this stage, lignin lost its free water, resulting in a decrease in mass, but the chemical structure of lignin remained intact. The second and third stages of pyrolysis occurred between 189.9 °C and 477.1 °C, with a total weight loss of 47.7 %. These stages correspond to the initial volatilization of lignin, where the pyrolysis products are complex and mainly include gases such as H2O, CH4, CO, and CO2. The weight loss during these stages is due to the cleavage of methoxy groups connected to the benzene rings in lignin, producing small molecules like CO and CH4. Additionally, the breakdown of functional groups and side chains, such as -OH, -COOH, and C-C bonds in the phenyl rings, releases hydrocarbon gases and oxygenated compounds like H2O, CO, and CO2. The fourth stage of pyrolysis occurs above 477.1 °C, with a weight loss of 28.4 %. This stage corresponds to the deep pyrolysis of lignin, primarily resulting in the release of CO and small amount of CO2. The weight loss is attributed to the pyrolysis of aromatic compounds and the cleavage of ether bonds between phenylpropane units [55].

The GPC results of lignin product are shown in Fig. 3 C. Based on the data analysis, the number-average molecular weight (Mn) of the lignin is 477 g/mol, the weight-average molecular weight (Mw) is 1084 g/mol, and the polydispersity index (D) is 2.27. Similar to other lignins extracted using DES, this lignin exhibits low Mn and index D. For instance, Li et al. applied a DES using choline chloride and p-toluenesulfonic acid to extract lignin, which had a Mw of 678 g/mol, Mn of 603 g/mol, and index D of 1.12 [56]. The relatively low molecular weight of the obtained lignin suggests that some degree of molecular chain breakage occurs during extraction. Besides that, the molecular weight distribution curve of the lignin exhibits a single peak, indicating a relatively uniform molecular structure.

Furthermore, the XPS full spectrum in Fig. 3 D indicates that the lignin contains a substantial amount of carbon (C) and oxygen (O), along with a small amount of nitrogen (N). Elemental peak area integration reveals the relative contents of C, O, and N to be 77.14 %, 20.9 %, and 1.70 %, respectively. As shown in the C 1 s XPS spectrum as Fig. 3 E, there are three different forms of carbon atoms. The carbon atoms with the lowest energy form C-C, CC, and C-H bonds, with a binding energy of 284.7 eV. Another type of carbon atom is bonded to a single oxygen atom, forming a C-O bond with a binding energy of 285.2 eV. A third type of carbon atom forms two bonds with oxygen atoms, including CO, O-C-O, and O-CO bonds, with binding energies of 286.6 eV and 288.7 eV, respectively. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3 F shows two main peaks; one is associated with C-O bonds at 532.0 eV, and the other corresponds to CO bonds at 533.2 eV. These results confirm that during DES treatment, some nitrogen from choline chloride is combined in the product, explaining the presence of nitrogen in the subsequent CDs synthesis.

The 1H NMR spectrum of lignin is shown in Fig. 3 G, with DMSO-d
6 as the solvent. The specific chemical shifts and their assignments are detailed in Table S2 (see SM). The spectrum reveals the peaks corresponding to the guaiacyl (G) and syringyl (S) units of lignin, with signals at 7.29–6.82 ppm and 6.82–6.32 ppm, respectively. This confirms that the lignin obtained from Siraitia grosvenorii shells is of the GS type [57]. In addition, the 13C NMR spectrum of lignin is illustrated in Fig. 3 H. Detailed assignments of the chemical shifts are provided in Table S3 (see SM). The spectrum shows signals in the range of 160–103 ppm, which correspond to the aromatic rings of lignin. The signals at 90–60 ppm are attributed to the C-α, C-β, and C-γ carbons in the β-O-4′ structures of S and G units, while the range of 90–20 ppm indicates aliphatic carbon signals [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of basic properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Here CDs were synthesized using the prepared lignin as a precursor through a hydrothermal synthesis reaction. Quinine sulfate was selected as a standard reference for the determination of quantum yield [58]; as the result, a quantum yield of 5.73 % was obtained for the synthesized CDs. As comparison, the quantum yields of CDs were in the range of 0.4–2.8 % from the raw materials of various wood species—Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica Litv., Calocedrus macrolepis var. formosana, Cunninghamia lanceolata (Lamb.) Hook., and Zenia insignis Chun [59]. Then the basic properties of the CDs were characterized, including nanoparticle size analysis, TEM, XPS, FTIR, and UV.

The full XPS spectrum is shown in Fig. 4 A, revealing that the CDs contain a significant amount of C, O, and N, with relative contents of 67.43 %, 24.36 %, and 8.21 %, respectively. A distinct Na Auger feature peak is also observed near 497 eV, suggesting the presence of abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs. The C 1 s XPS spectrum in Fig. 4 B displays prominent peaks at 284.7 eV, corresponding to C-C and CC bonds; 286.1 eV, indicative of C-N bonds; 286.6 eV, attributed to C-O bonds; and 287.9 eV, resulting from CO bonds. In the O 1 s spectrum as Fig. 4 C, peaks are observed at 532.2 eV, associated with CO bonds, and 531.1 eV, corresponding to C-O bonds. The N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 4 D shows peak positions at 402.4 eV, attributed to -CO-NH2/-NH- groups, and at 399.8 eV, originating from C-N-C/-NH2 bonds [60,61]. These data suggest that the surface of the CDs is rich in hydrophilic groups, which confer good water solubility. The nitrogen element is present on the surface of the CDs in the form of amino groups, which originate from the lignin used in the preparation of the CDs. This lignin was extracted using a DES of ChCl-Urea, with the nitrogen from the residual urea in the lignin being retained during the synthesis of the CDs.

Figs. 4E and 4F present the TEM images of the CDs, with the inset illustrating the size distribution of the CDs. It can be observed that the CDs exhibit a spherical morphology, with an average diameter of 2.75 nm, and they show a regular lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm. This proves that the CDs have formed a degree of regular molecular ordering. Furthermore, Fig. 4 G presents the measurement results from the nanoparticle size analyzer, which statistically determined the size of the CDs to be 4.01 nm. This discrepancy with the TEM results is attributed to differences in the sample preparation methods used during the measurements. Compared to other CDs prepared from lignin, their size is relatively small. For example, Xu et al. prepared CDs with a size of about 10 nm from bamboo lignin, and CDs prepared by Henn et al. showed a size of around 30 nm using the chemo-enzymatic method [55,62].

Finally, spectral analysis was performed on the product of CDs. The FTIR results are depicted in Fig. 4 H. The CDs exhibit signals at 3500 cm−1 attributed to hydroxyl and amine groups, a peak at 2900 cm−1 associated with C-H stretching vibrations, and signals in the range of 1800–1600 cm−1 indicative of CO vibrations. The peak at 1735 cm−1 correlates with α, β-unsaturated carboxylic acid groups, while the signal at 1630 cm−1 is likely due to an amide bond. The low wavenumber signals suggest that the CDs possess an aromatic structure, similar to the carbon core of graphene [63,64]. As shown in the UV spectrum of the CDs as Fig. 4 I, the conjugated systems within the CDs typically exhibit strong absorbance in the UV region (230–320 nm), which primarily consists of π-π* and n-π* transitions. Differential processing of the absorption spectrum of the CDs from 230 to 330 nm revealed distinct absorption peaks at 270 nm and 317 nm, indicating characteristic absorbance within the 230–320 nm range. In the UV region, the CDs have high absorbance intensity but weak peak values, possibly due to their complex structure and aromatic functional groups. Above 350 nm, the absorbance is broad and of low intensity, which is associated with the low-energy band gap formed by the functional groups on the surface of the CDs [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the basic fluorescent properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the basic fluorescent properties of the CDs were characterized, with a focus primarily on their emission and excitation spectra, as well as the variation in the emission spectrum in response to changes in the concentration and pH of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of the CDs was measured at an excitation wavelength of 460 nm, while the photoluminescence excitation spectrum was recorded over excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 480 nm in increments of 20 nm. The excitation spectrum reveals a strong peak for the CDs at 377 nm, with a Stokes shift of 83 nm.

The emission spectrum curve indicates that the CDs exhibit tunable multicolor fluorescence. At an excitation wavelength of 300 nm, the maximum emission wavelength is observed at approximately 440 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases to 320 nm, the maximum emission wavelength blue-shifts to 430 nm. When the excitation wavelength reaches 340 nm, the maximum emission wavelength red-shifts to 435 nm. Further increasing the excitation wavelength from 340 nm to 480 nm results in a red-shift of the maximum emission wavelength from 435 nm to 540 nm. The maximum emission intensity initially increases and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength, peaking at 380 nm. CDs synthesized from lignin usually exhibit this fluorescence tunability. For example, Wang et al. prepared CDs from papaya, and by changing the excitation wavelength in the range of 350–490 nm, the emission peak shifted from 445 nm to 530 nm [65].

The relationship between the emission peaks and fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the range of 300–500 nm was analyzed. Figs. 5 B and 5 C show that within the excitation wavelength range of 315–335 nm, the maximum emission wavelength remains essentially unchanged, while it significantly depends on the excitation wavelength in other ranges. This behavior is in accordance with the Kasha-Vavilov rule and is possibly caused by quantum conjugation effects [63]. Fig. 5 D illustrates the relationship between the fluorescence emission intensity and the excitation wavelength of the CDs, with the maximum excitation wavelength occurring at approximately 380 nm. The red-shift of the excitation wavelength for the CDs is related to nitrogen doping, which increases the carrier concentration and lowers the Fermi level, thus enabling the excitation of the CDs by lower-energy light sources [66].

The prepared CDs exhibit distinct pH-responsive luminescent properties. As shown in Fig. 5 E, the CDs demonstrate enhanced fluorescence emission in acidic environments, with the highest intensity observed at their original pH value of 2.90. The fluorescence intensity peaks when the pH is slightly increased to nearly 4. However, under alkaline conditions, while the fluorescence of the CDs does not completely extinguish, there is a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity, which further decreases as the alkalinity increases. As proved in Fig. 5 F, the concentration of CDs can significantly affect their photoluminescent properties, including both the emission wavelength and intensity. An increase in the concentration of CDs leads to a red-shift in the maximum emission wavelength, likely due to the self-assembly of molecules. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission intensity increases with the concentration of CDs, but there is a slight decrease when the concentration is increased from a twofold dilution to undiluted. This decrease is attributed to the negative impact of self-assembly at higher concentrations on fluorescence emission.

Moreover, the stability of CDs under various solvent conditions was evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 5 G, the fluorescence response consistently exhibits a linear decrease across four different solvents as the pH is increased from 4 to 10, suggesting that the CDs maintain a consistent fluorescence emission behavior in all tested solvents. These findings indicate that the CDs consistently generate fluorescence across various solvent environments, suggesting favorable stability. Furthermore, Fig. 5 H displays the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs solution obtained from repeated experiments, showing that the five parallel groups had nearly identical spectra with tiny variation in peak positions and low error rates, confirming the repeatability of the fluorescence experiments for reused CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the quenching and recovery mechanisms of CDs fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 6 A, the impact of various metal ions on the fluorescence of CDs was assessed, revealing that Fe3+ nearly completely quenches the fluorescence of CDs. In addition to Fe3+, the metal ions of Cu2+, Co2+, and Ni2+ were also found to exert a quenching effect to a certain extent. The selectivity of CDs as fluorescence probes for detecting metal ions, particularly for various coexisting metal cations, is one of the most important issues when applying them to real-world samples. Similarly, it has been found CDs derived from green sources typically exhibit strong selectivity for Fe3+, with even small amounts of Fe3+ causing a significant quenching effect, while other metal ions do not exhibit such a pronounced effect [67–69]. For example, the CDs prepared by Wang et al. from papaya show a strong selective response to Fe3+, with some response to Co2+, Hg2+, and Ni2+, but much weaker than that for Fe3+ [65]. The selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ is attributed to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, such as -OH and -COOH, which enable CDs to serve as probes for detecting transition metals. As shown in Fig. 6 B, the effect of varying concentrations of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of CDs is demonstrated. It was observed that even a small addition of Fe3+ can lead to significant fluorescence quenching, and at a concentration of 0.01 mol/L, the fluorescence is completely quenched. This is likely due to the binding of iron ions with CDs, forming a CDs/Fe3+ complex, which results in strong fluorescence quenching due to electron transfer within the complex. Furthermore, a red-shift of the maximum emission wavelength occurs as the concentration of iron ions increases, which is also attributed to the complexation of Fe3+ with CDs.

The fluorescence effect of CDs can be specifically influenced by certain substances, leading to changes in their fluorescence properties, such as with toxoflavin and formaldehyde [70,71]. In this study, four types of DESs were selected and mixed with the CDs solution to investigate their impact on the fluorescence effect of the CDs. Fig. 6 C–6 F illustrated the comparative effects of four types of DESs and their individual components on the fluorescence of CDs. In the ChCl-Mon group, the addition of monoethanolamine leads to a decrease in emission intensity, while choline chloride has almost no effect. In the ChCl-Urea group, neither choline chloride nor urea significantly affects the emission intensity. For the Lys-Gly group, the combination of Lys and Gly notably enhances the emission intensity, whereas neither lysine nor glycerol alone has a significant impact. In the ChCl-Imi group, the presence of imidazole results in a reduction of emission intensity, while choline chloride remains largely unaffected.

After the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by Fe3+, the addition of four types of DESs resulted in some recovery, and this study investigated the restorative effects of these four DESs on the fluorescence of CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 G, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched upon the addition of Fe3+. Subsequently, the addition of four types of DESs, namely ChCl-Mon (1:7), ChCl-Imi (3:7), ChCl-Urea (1:2), and Lys-Gly (1:8), reveals that except for the ChCl-Urea group, the other three DESs can restore some of the quenched fluorescence to varying degrees. There are several possible reasons for this observation: firstly, the DES solutions are alkaline, and the OH- in the solution reacts with Fe3+, thereby eliminating the Fe3+ ions complexed with CDs and leading to the recovery of fluorescence; secondly, the components within the DES can form complexes with Fe3+, preventing Fe3+ from complexing with CDs and thus allowing the fluorescence to recover. Finally, after the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by different metal ions, ChCl-Mon was employed to recover the fluorescence. The results, as depicted in Fig. 6 H, show that following the addition of ChCl-Mon, the fluorescence of CDs solutions containing Ni2+, Co2+, and Cu2+ remained diminished, whereas only the fluorescence of CDs containing Fe3+ was restored. This indicates that this method exhibits specificity for Fe3+. In summary, Table S4 in SM includes detailed comparison on the reported CDs and CDs in this study for Fe3+ detecting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as fluorescent probes for detecting the concentration of DES
ce:section.ce:para: DESs are widely applied across various fields, including electrodeposition, extraction and separation, catalysis, and nanomaterials. However, efficient and practical methods for detecting residual DESs in their solution remain limited. For example, Ossi and colleagues developed a selective oil-removing aerogel pretreated with a choline chloride-based DES[72], and the decline trend of reusability of aerogel sorbent has indicated the potential abscission of DES. However, its released amount in the oil-water system has not been determined. In similar studies, most of the DESs used during pretreatment were recovered, though a small proportion remained in the aqueous phase and was subsequently discharged with wastewater. In this work, two analytical methods were established to quantify DES concentrations for comparison and validation. These techniques enable rapid detection of DESs in related samples through fluorescence signaling, supporting accurate quantification of residual levels and enhancing the dealing with environment-healthy-safety (EHS) issues.

Two methods were attempted for the determination of DES concentration. The first method involved the preparation of a CDs/Fe3+ solution as a fluorescent probe for the measurement; and the second method utilized a direct CDs solution as a fluorescent probe for the determination, with the DES selected for detection being ChCl-Imi. Currently, CDs are commonly used to detect the content of various substances by either combining CDs with other materials or utilizing the interaction between CDs and metal ions for direct detection of harmful metal ions. For example, CDs@ZIF-L/PEI composites have been constructed to measure the concentration of Hg2+ [73]; Tb@CDs/GSH-CuNCs composites have been used to detect phosphate anions [74]; and cadmium sulfide quantum dots have been synthesized for the detection of tetracycline in wastewater [75]. CDs derived from biological waste have been directly employed to detect metal ions such as Fe3+, Hg2+, and Cu2+ [76]. Up to now, no approach has been reported where the fluorescence quenching of CDs by metal ions is followed by the addition of other substances to measure the fluorescence recovery for detection. This study aims to explore and develop this novel approach.

As depicted in Fig. 7 A, upon the addition of varying concentrations of ChCl-Imi to the CDs/Fe3+ solution, followed by filtration to precipitate, the changes in fluorescence intensity were observed. The graph also illustrates the color change of the detection solvent as the concentration of ChCl-Imi increases. There are three distinct stages of change observed: in the first stage, the DES-induced decrease in pH value leads to a reduction in fluorescence intensity; in the second stage, as the pH reaches the precipitation condition for Fe3+, the concentration of Fe3+ decreases, resulting in a recovery of fluorescence intensity; in the third stage, with Fe3+ fully precipitated, further addition of DES causes a decrease in pH, leading to a subsequent reduction in fluorescence intensity. The conclusion is that the variation in fluorescence intensity of the CDs/Fe3+ system is only applicable within a narrow concentration range of 0.03–0.04 mol/L for DES. However, due to this limited range, it is not suitable for use as a detection reagent.

As shown in Fig. 7 B, the graph illustrates the variation in the maximum fluorescence intensity of CDs upon the addition of different concentrations of DES. It can be observed from the graph that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs continuously decreases as the DES concentration increases. Initially, upon the addition of DES, there is a rapid decline in fluorescence intensity. On the other hand, the rate of decrease in fluorescence intensity becomes less steep as the concentration of DES continues to increase, which is attributed to the rapid change in the alkalinity of the solution upon the initial addition of DES. However, the pH change becomes less pronounced as more DES is added, leading to a slower change in fluorescence intensity. Through the analysis on the graph, it appears that for the detection of DES concentration using CDs, a more appropriate range would be within 0–0.5 mol/L. If the concentration of DES continues to increase beyond this range, the sensitivity of the CDs to DES concentration decreases, resulting in reduced detection accuracy. The variation curve was fitted using the ExpDec2 model, with the fitting formula shown in this figure. The R² value is 0.9983, indicating an ideal fitting result; and the fitted curve can serve as a standard curve for subsequent concentration determination. A comparative assessment was performed to evaluate the efficacy of HPLC and CDs methods for the determination of ChCl-Imi. Fig. 7 C depicts the standard calibration curve for the HPLC method, which is defined by the equation of y = 13654 + 135484x with the R2 value of 0.9800. Table 1 presents a comparative analysis between the two methodologies. It was observed that the spike recovery rates for the CDs method consistently fell within the range of 95–105 %, aligning with the standards required for DES concentration detection. Moreover, the CDs method offers a more rapid determination process compared to the HPLC method and eliminates the need for certain organic solvents, rendering the approach more environmentally sustainable.

In this study, DESs were first used as green solvent in the pretreatment and preparation for lignin from Siraitia grosvenorii shells, which was then used to synthesize carbon dots and ultimately detect trace DES. A compositional analysis of raw material revealed that their main components were cellulose and lignin, accounting for 34.7 % and 29.8 %, respectively. After that, DESs showed satisfied performance to remove lipids and extract lignin from the discarded shells of Siraitia grosvenorii, and the most effective extractant system was explored as ChCl-Lac, achieving an extraction yield of 92.49 %. The extracted lignin was comprehensively characterized using FTIR, XPS, TG NMR, and GPC, revealing that it was a GS-type lignin.

Subsequently, the lignin product was employed as a raw material to synthesize CDs with tunable fluorescence properties, achieving a quantum yield of 5.73 %. The CDs were comprehensively characterized using FTIR, TEM, XPS, UV, and nanoparticle size analysis, with a particular focus on their fluorescence properties. Their fluorescence responses to various metal ions and DESs were discovered. It was found that Fe3+ exhibited a significant fluorescence quenching effect on the CDs, and certain DESs also influenced the fluorescence behavior of the CDs. Based on these findings, a new strategy was developed using CDs as a fluorescent probe for detecting DES concentrations in aqueous solutions. Compared with traditional HPLC method, this approach offers advantages such as easier implement, faster detection speed and a more friendly process. It not only expands the potential applications of natural biomass resources but also provides an effective method for detecting DES for those concerned issues in basic researches and actual applications.
